does: This paper extends mechanistic interpretability toward forward engineering by introducing a concept bottleneck framework for time series transformers in long-term forecasting. The authors modify the training objective using Centered Kernel Alignment to encourage models to develop representations aligned with predefined, interpretable concepts while allowing other components freedom to learn undefined patterns. They apply this framework to three transformer architectures (Vanilla Transformer, Autoformer, FEDformer) and demonstrate through experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets that the approach substantially improves interpretability without significantly degrading forecasting performance. The paper validates interpretations through activation patching intervention experiments, confirming that bottleneck components genuinely capture the intended concepts.
connects: This work builds on the concept bottleneck model literature, which has been applied to vision and other domains to constrain neural networks to use human-interpretable intermediate representations. It extends prior mechanistic interpretability research, which typically focuses on reverse engineering learned mechanisms after training, by instead forward engineering interpretability constraints into the learning process. The paper also draws on recent work in time series forecasting with transformers, particularly advances like Autoformer and FEDformer that improve upon vanilla transformers for this task. The use of Centered Kernel Alignment to align representations with predefined concepts extends existing alignment techniques from representation learning. The intervention methodology via activation patching connects to causal interpretation methods in neural networks.
enables: This framework enables future work toward more transparent and trustworthy time series forecasting systems, particularly in high-stakes domains like finance and climate modeling where interpretability is crucial. The approach could generalize beyond time series to other sequential prediction tasks, enabling broader application of concept bottleneck constraints in temporal modeling. The success of maintaining performance while improving interpretability suggests future work could explore learning concept dictionaries automatically rather than predefining them, or applying similar forward-engineering approaches to other architecture types. The intervention validation methodology could inform development of more rigorous evaluation protocols for interpretability in deep learning. The framework also points toward hybrid systems combining learned and human-defined concepts, potentially enabling better human-AI collaboration in forecasting applications.
